# Questions in Livestream 211

Live Stream #211: When Your Guest Is A Gas Bag...

Time links open the YouTube video at the relevant timestamp.

| Time | Question | Short answer / answer direction | Expanded answer |
|---:|---|---|---|
| <a href="https://youtu.be/-moIej_LjtE?t=520" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">8:40</a> | Given the clan interpretation, how should one interpret Noah's 120 years to build the ark in Genesis 6:3? | The 120 years is treated as a countdown to the flood rather than the building period. Under a clan reading, Noah's household or posterity could also be involved in the ark work. | Falk reads Genesis 6:3 as God setting a 120-year limit before judgment, not as a statement that Noah personally spent that whole span building. He notes that older interpretations sometimes treated the number as a post-flood lifespan limit, but he favors the countdown-to-flood reading. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/-moIej_LjtE?t=823" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">13:43</a> | Do other ancient Near Eastern nations put themselves in the harsh light Israel does when it turns its back on Yahweh? | Not normally. Other nations might blame a defeated king or accuse enemies of abandoning gods, but Falk says whole-nation self-rebuke is not typical. | Falk says he has not seen other nations characteristically rebuke themselves the way Israel does. He allows that successors might blame a defeated king for not obeying the gods, or conquerors might accuse enemies of abandoning their gods, but he distinguishes that from a whole nation indicting itself for apostasy. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/-moIej_LjtE?t=963" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">16:03</a> | Did other ancient Near Eastern nations have a national deity as involved as Yahweh, directly interacting in history? | Sometimes victories were credited to gods intervening, but defeats were not usually blamed on the nation's own disobedience to its deity; Falk treats that as distinctive to Israel. | Falk says ancient rulers could credit victories to divine intervention, using Ramesses II at Kadesh as an example of a king attributing success to gods strengthening him. The contrast is that they did not normally say their own defeat came because their deity judged their disobedience, which Falk treats as an Israelite distinctiveness. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/-moIej_LjtE?t=1089" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">18:09</a> | Do other ancient Eastern faiths depict their deities as directly giving laws or worship to their nations? | Not typically. Egypt worked by a principle of order rather than a law code, while Mesopotamian law was descriptive precedent rather than prescriptive legislation. | Falk contrasts societies ordered by law with societies ordered by order. He says Egypt did not have a formal law code in the relevant sense, so its laws were not presented as divinely given, while Mesopotamian collections recorded judgments and precedents rather than functioning like prescriptive covenant law. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/-moIej_LjtE?t=1281" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">21:21</a> | Is the abandonment of Avaris in the mid-19th dynasty good evidence for the Exodus, and does BTAC make that connection? | Falk says it is good corroborating evidence, but BTAC does not make that connection because he ties the Exodus to the mid-12th century through the four-room house evidence. | Falk considers the near abandonment of Avaris, except for the port, in the mid-19th dynasty to be good corroborating evidence because that is where he expects the Exodus event. He says BTAC does not follow that connection, preferring a mid-12th-century date tied to four-room houses, and Falk criticizes that for not fitting the Merneptah Stele reference to Israel. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/-moIej_LjtE?t=1486" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">24:46</a> | Do you find super-late daters or early daters worse? | He finds early daters more frustrating because they generally work from less evidence and often begin from a fixed traditional position, though super-late daters also ignore key evidence. | Falk says super-late daters ignore important facts, but early daters frustrate him more because they seem to him to be working with less evidence and hoping the early date will work. He then defines the super-late view as the mid-12th-century line associated with Cyrus Gordon and later holdouts such as Rendsburg and BTAC. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/-moIej_LjtE?t=1700" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">28:20</a> | What are your views on Douglas Petrovich's 2013 argument for pinpointing the Egyptian abandonment of Avaris in the mid-18th dynasty? | Falk rejects it strongly. He says the palace abandonment does not show the whole city was abandoned, because Asiatic occupation continued in the temple district into the 19th dynasty. | Falk calls the paper badly flawed because it treats the abandonment of Amenhotep II's Egyptian palace as if it meant Avaris itself was abandoned. He says the palace was Egyptian, not Asiatic, and that Semitic occupation continued elsewhere at the site, especially the temple district, so the argument cannot establish a mid-18th-dynasty Exodus. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/-moIej_LjtE?t=1963" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">32:43</a> | What are the urban population estimates for Avaris in the 18th to 19th dynasty? | Falk cites BTAC's estimate of roughly 5,000 to 50,000 people, based on pottery production, while noting the range is very broad. | Falk gives BTAC's estimate as 5,000 to 50,000 people and says it was based on pottery production at the site. He emphasizes that the estimate is quite broad and therefore only gives a rough sense of scale rather than a precise population count. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/-moIej_LjtE?t=2003" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">33:23</a> | Why is Amenhotep II the focus point for so many Exodus timelines? | Falk traces it to James Orr's early-date argument and later attempts to identify Amenhotep II as the Exodus pharaoh, but he says those arguments depend on unsupported claims about the oppression pharaoh. | Falk traces the focus to James Orr's 1906 defense of an early Exodus date around 1450 BC and the resulting identification of Amenhotep II as the Exodus pharaoh. He says later supporters tried to justify that by tying the oppression pharaoh to Thutmose III's long reign, but he treats the move as driven by the theory rather than by strong evidence. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/-moIej_LjtE?t=2342" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">39:02</a> | Would the wandering period not be exactly, or even roughly, 40 years? | Falk says it is not exact; the number functions as a vague or idiomatic estimate, so it could be near 40 without being precisely 40. | Falk says the 40 years should not be pressed as an exact chronological figure. He treats it as a vague estimate or idiom, meaning it could be 39, 42, or otherwise close to the stated span without requiring mathematical precision. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/-moIej_LjtE?t=2413" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">40:13</a> | In Genesis 9:20-23, what did Ham do wrong? Was it voyeurism or a euphemism involving Noah's wife? | Falk argues it was not mere voyeurism. He reads "uncovering nakedness" as a euphemism involving Noah's wife, while cautioning that the woman was not necessarily Ham's mother. | Falk argues that simple voyeurism does not explain why Noah would wake up and know what had been done to him. He reads the language about nakedness as a euphemism tied to a sexual offense involving Noah's wife, but he cautions against assuming the woman must have been Ham's own mother. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/-moIej_LjtE?t=2925" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">48:45</a> | What is the meaning of Psalm 14, especially "the fool has said in his heart there is no God"? | Falk says the phrase can mean either denying God's existence or acting as if God is not present to judge. The Psalm begins with the fool but broadens to humanity's need for redemption. | Falk says the question is broad because even the opening verse contains several interpretive issues. He explains that "there is no God" may mean atheistic denial or practical denial that God is present to judge, then notes that the psalm moves from the fool's corruption to a broader indictment that no one does good. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/-moIej_LjtE?t=3233" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">53:53</a> | What are your thoughts on icons and icon veneration? | He calls icon veneration an accretion: abiblical but not necessarily unbiblical. He doubts its efficacy while distinguishing that from saying it is against Scripture. | Falk defines an accretion as a practice added later rather than beginning with the early church. He says icon veneration is abiblical because Scripture does not teach it, but not necessarily unbiblical or contrary to Scripture; his main reservation is whether the practice actually accomplishes anything. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/-moIej_LjtE?t=3462" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">57:42</a> | In Romans 11:26, who is meant by Israel? | Falk reads Israel as the Israelites, contrasted with Gentiles. Once the fullness of the Gentiles comes in, the partial hardening is lifted and the Israelite remnant comes to salvation. | Falk reads Romans 11:25-26 together and says the contrast with Gentiles clarifies that Israel means Israelites. He explains the "mystery" as a revealed secret: Israel has a partial hardening until the fullness of the Gentiles comes in, after which the remnant of Israel is saved. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/-moIej_LjtE?t=4215" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:10:15</a> | Does the Quran's claim that Egyptians practiced crucifixion as capital punishment fit the images of people on vertical and horizontal posts at Kom Ombo? | Falk says crucifixion in Egypt is a Hellenistic import, not a native Egyptian practice. He says Kom Ombo is a Ptolemaic temple and earlier Egyptian capital punishments were different. | Falk says crucifixion entered Egypt through the Greeks, with a chain of transmission from Assyrians to Persians to Greeks and then Romans. Because Kom Ombo is Ptolemaic, he does not treat it as evidence for native Egyptian crucifixion, and he identifies earlier Egyptian capital punishments as strangulation and throat cutting. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/-moIej_LjtE?t=4456" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:14:16</a> | Were the Hebrews only in Avaris, or did they also settle in the larger area of Goshen? | Falk says the Semitic Asiatics who became Israel were spread through the Goshen area, including Avaris and surrounding pasture regions, with most in the Nile Delta. | Falk places the Semitic Asiatics who became Israel throughout Goshen, including Avaris, the surrounding pasture land, and the Wadi Tumilat area near Pithom. He adds that some Asiatics may have been elsewhere in Egypt, even as far south as the Theban region, but says the vast majority were in the Delta. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/-moIej_LjtE?t=4592" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:16:32</a> | Were Israelites at Elephantine Island during the Exodus period? | Falk says no; he places Jewish presence at Elephantine later, after Nebuchadnezzar's conquest and the assassination of Gedaliah. | Falk rejects an Exodus-period Israelite presence at Elephantine. He places Jewish movement there after Nebuchadnezzar's destruction of Jerusalem and the assassination of Gedaliah, then explains the island's Egyptian name as tied to ivory trade rather than to the Exodus setting. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/-moIej_LjtE?t=4745" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:19:05</a> | If Matthew 10:28 says the soul is destructible while Matthew 25 and Revelation speak of eternal fire, what is the second death and do we cease to be? | Falk rejects annihilationism here. He reads "destroy" as bring to ruin, and the second death as final separation from God rather than ceasing to exist. | Falk starts with Matthew 10:28 and argues that "destroy" means to bring to ruin, not to erase from existence. He says annihilationism imports a modern idea into the text, and he frames the second death as final relational ruin and separation from God rather than the soul ceasing to be. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/-moIej_LjtE?t=5625" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:33:45</a> | Is there no postmortem or postjudgment salvation? | Falk says not for those who had a fair choice and knowingly rejected God. He leaves room for grace where there was no real choice, but says judgment closes the ordinary option. | Falk leaves open the possibility of grace in cases where a person had no real choice, but says that where someone knowingly rejected God after a fair choice, judgment closes the option. He connects that to a lack of desire for repentance, forgiveness, holiness, or salvation after the person has chosen to bear the consequences. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/-moIej_LjtE?t=5705" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:35:05</a> | Why would people change their minds after rejecting God while willingly knowing the truth? | Falk says they would not; if someone knows the truth and chooses to reject it anyway, there is no reason to expect a later change of mind. | Falk agrees with the premise of the question: if people knew the truth and still chose rejection, there is no reason to expect a later reversal. The answer functions as part of his explanation for why postjudgment salvation is not expected for those who knowingly rejected God. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/-moIej_LjtE?t=5740" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:35:40</a> | Does eternal life mean more than eternal existence? | Yes. Falk distinguishes eternal life as Zoe, fullness and quality of life in relationship, from mere bios or existence; eternal death is eternal relational brokenness. | Falk says annihilationists define life too narrowly as mere existence. He describes eternal life as abundant living, growth, community, and restored relationships with God and others; by contrast, eternal death is ongoing brokenness in those relationships rather than nonexistence. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/-moIej_LjtE?t=6142" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:42:22</a> | How did you calculate the 2.5% tax figure for ancient Israel? | Falk says it comes from the Talmud and Jewish historical tradition about tax burdens from the monarchic period into the Herodian period, while noting the Talmud is not always correct. | Falk says the 2.5% figure is drawn from the Talmud and Jewish tradition about taxation from the monarchic kingdom into the Herodian period. He cautions that the Talmud is not always right, but says it is the best evidence he has for that taxation question. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/-moIej_LjtE?t=6205" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:43:25</a> | How can Hebrew be so versatile that one translator renders a word as drift and another as bentness or brokenness? | He explains this through lexical range. Hebrew has a small vocabulary, so words often carry broader semantic ranges than languages with more lexemes. | Falk explains the variation through lexical scope or lexical range, the set of possible meanings a word can carry. He says Hebrew has comparatively few lexemes, so words often do double duty across broad ranges, whereas a language with more lexemes can assign narrower meanings to individual words. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/-moIej_LjtE?t=6837" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:53:57</a> | How long have you been on the super-soldier serum, given your work ethic and past jobs? | He answers humorously, then says he gets a lot done largely because he does not own or watch television and uses that time for work. | Falk answers the "serum" framing jokingly, then gives a practical explanation for his productivity. He says he does not own a television, barely watches movies, and often works even when watching something, so the time others spend on television becomes work time for him. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/-moIej_LjtE?t=7222" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:00:22</a> | Is the Shekinah God's spirit or word? | Falk equates the Shekinah glory with the Holy Spirit: the presence or essence of God within holy space such as the temple or tabernacle. | Falk says the Shekinah glory corresponds to God's Holy Spirit rather than to a separate "word" category in this question. He describes it as the essence or presence of God within holy space, specifically the temple or tabernacle. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/-moIej_LjtE?t=7279" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:01:19</a> | How are Moody's language programs? | He says the language programs are okay, but strongly warns against Moody because he views the school as legalistic and restrictive. | Falk separates the language programs from the school culture: he says the programs are okay, but he would not recommend Moody because of what he describes as fundamentalist and legalistic rules. He cites grooming rules, curfew, dress expectations, filtered media, and a high deconversion rate as reasons for concern. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/-moIej_LjtE?t=7546" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:05:46</a> | Is Moody's restrictive policy structure even legal? | Falk says yes because attendance is voluntary. He calls the policies shortsighted and unhealthy, but says they can be legal in that setting. | Falk says the restrictions can be legal because students voluntarily attend and the setting is private. He distinguishes legality from wisdom, calling the policies shortsighted and harmful, and argues that administrators keep the structure because of donor pressure despite negative outcomes. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/-moIej_LjtE?t=7822" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:10:22</a> | Did Ramesses II actually get a passport? | Yes. Falk explains that a passport or transport document was issued so the mummy could be transported to Paris for conservation under European rules for human remains. | Falk says Ramesses II did receive passport-style documentation because European rules for transporting human remains required identification to prevent trafficking concerns. The document allowed the mummy to be transported to Paris for conservation, though he clarifies jokingly that the caretakers handled the paperwork. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/-moIej_LjtE?t=7923" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:12:03</a> | In Deuteronomy 21:3-4, why that kind of heifer, and is the neck broken because blood was already spilled? | Falk says the ritual addresses bloodguilt on the land when a murderer is unknown. The unused heifer is ritually pure and serves as a costly absolution ceremony for communal innocence. | Falk explains the scenario as an unsolved murder in open country where the nearest town must address bloodguilt on the land. The heifer has never worked, pulled a yoke, or given birth, so it is an unused and costly animal; the ceremony declares communal innocence and seeks atonement for the polluted land. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/-moIej_LjtE?t=8270" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:17:50</a> | How does Deuteronomy 21:15-16 relate to Jacob's treatment of Joseph and Reuben? | Falk says the law closes a loophole Jacob used. Jacob effectively gave Joseph the firstborn double portion through Ephraim and Manasseh, even though Reuben was the biological firstborn. | Falk says Deuteronomy 21 closes a patriarchal loophole by forbidding a father from choosing the favored wife's son over the actual firstborn unless the firstborn disqualifies himself. He explains that Jacob effectively made Joseph the firstborn by giving Ephraim and Manasseh the double portion that would otherwise belong to Reuben. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/-moIej_LjtE?t=8653" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:24:13</a> | In Deuteronomy 21:18, what kind of rebelliousness is in view? | Falk says this is not a disobedient child but an adult son engaged in serious ritual and social rebellion, such as idolatry, violence, theft, drunkenness, and gluttony that harms others. | Falk emphasizes that the passage is about an adult son, not a small child who failed to obey parents. He defines the rebellion as severe ritual and social rebellion: idolatry, violence, theft, drunkenness, gluttony, and similar conduct so serious it cannot be handled by an ordinary offering. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/-moIej_LjtE?t=8939" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:28:59</a> | Does 1 Corinthians 1:13 mean they baptized in the name of Jesus? | Yes, but Falk says Paul's point is that baptism by Paul, Apollos, Cephas, or others should not create factions because all are baptized in Christ. | Falk says Christians were baptized in Jesus' name, but Paul's point in 1 Corinthians is not to establish a baptismal formula. Paul is confronting factionalism: people were using association with Paul, Apollos, Cephas, or even Christ as a claim of exclusivity, despite Christ not being divided. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/-moIej_LjtE?t=9233" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:33:53</a> | Why is partialism a heresy if infinite parts of God would still be infinite? | Falk says the issue is fullness of essence. Each person of the Trinity must have the fullness of the divine essence, not merely an infinite part of it. | Falk says the problem is not whether a divided part could still be called infinite, but whether each person has the fullness of divine essence. The Father, Son, and Spirit must each be fully God; if each has only a slice or third of the essence, that is not the traditional doctrine of the Trinity. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/-moIej_LjtE?t=9427" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:37:07</a> | Why was Eve given her name after the fall in Genesis 3:20 rather than when she was first created? | Falk says the text does not explain fully, but Eve's name is tied to being mother of the living, which fits after expulsion and the beginning of offspring; she may also have had another earlier name. | Falk says the text does not directly explain why the naming happens there. Since Eve's name is connected with life and being mother of the living, he suggests the timing fits after expulsion and the start of offspring, while also allowing that she may have had another name before this point. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/-moIej_LjtE?t=9955" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:45:55</a> | Do modern Hebrew learners need to worry about second tenses? | No. Falk says modern Hebrew does not have second tenses, and even in classical Hebrew they are rare and not fully understood. | Falk says modern Hebrew has no second tenses, so learners do not need to worry about them. He adds that second tenses tend to disappear over time in languages, giving Egyptian as an analogy, and that even classical Hebrew has them rarely enough that their function is not fully clear. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/-moIej_LjtE?t=10036" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:47:16</a> | Have you cosplayed, and if so, as what character? | He says he cosplayed once in college as Larva from Vampire Princess Miyu, and also did Renaissance fairs, though he does not count those as cosplay. | Falk says he cosplayed once as an undergraduate, choosing Larva from Vampire Princess Miyu. He mentions Renaissance fairs as a related costume activity but does not count them as cosplay, and says he no longer has photos because it was decades earlier. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/-moIej_LjtE?t=10273" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:51:13</a> | What are your favorite hard and soft cheeses? | He names Chaource, Cambozola, and Manchego among his favorite hard cheeses and says he likes ricotta among soft cheeses. | Falk first clarifies cheese categories, saying some cheeses commonly thought of as soft, such as brie, are technically not soft in the same way as ricotta or cream cheese. He then names Chaource, Manchego, and a German brie-like blue-veined cheese, identified as Cambozola, among favorites, with ricotta as a soft-cheese example. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/-moIej_LjtE?t=10496" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:54:56</a> | Have you ever had shawarma? | Yes, once. He found it unremarkable and prefers gyros, especially because he likes the spiced lamb and tzatziki. | Falk says he tried beef shawarma once after bringing it home from a local place and found it dry and unremarkable. He prefers gyros because he likes the lamb, the spicing, and the sauce better, even though he recognizes shawarma and gyros as similar wrapped meat dishes. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/-moIej_LjtE?t=10690" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:58:10</a> | Why is the genealogy so different between Matthew 1 and Luke 3:23-4? | Falk says they serve different purposes: one gives the legal/adoptive claim through the father and the other the genetic claim through the mother, intersecting at points. | Falk says the difference comes from what each genealogy is trying to accomplish. One line gives the legal or adoptive claim through the father, while the other gives the genetic claim through the mother, and he notes that the lines intersect at least at Shealtiel before continuing along related lines. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/-moIej_LjtE?t=10782" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:59:42</a> | How do we know the killing of males would last throughout the pharaoh's reign? | He clarifies that it would not necessarily last through a long reign, but in the relevant case the king's short reign would limit how long the decree operated. | Falk corrects the premise by saying the killing of males would not necessarily continue throughout a long reign. In the case he has in mind, the king's reign was only about four years, so if the decree began in the first or second year, it would last only a couple of years and be limited by the reign's short length. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/-moIej_LjtE?t=10879" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:01:19</a> | What do you think of the grammatical-historical-literal hermeneutic? | Falk likes the grammatical-historical method and calls it reliable, but says it is not necessarily literalist because genre, history, grammar, and context shape interpretation. | Falk endorses the grammatical-historical method but separates it from strict literalism. He says proper interpretation compares a text with history, grammar, genre, context, and historical associations; therefore poetry should be interpreted as poetry even while using a grammatical-historical method. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/-moIej_LjtE?t=11112" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:05:12</a> | How semantically hazy are Greek participles in the genitive case? | He says middle-voice Greek participles in the genitive are among the hardest cases and require context; without context, the word alone is not enough. | Falk says genitive Greek participles, especially middle-voice participles, are among the hardest interpretive cases and a nightmare for exegesis students. He stresses that context is everything; without a surrounding context, the form by itself is not enough to determine meaning. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/-moIej_LjtE?t=11282" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:08:02</a> | What are your thoughts on Luke's historical account when commentaries claim discrepancies? | Falk recommends using Kurt Aland's Synopsis of the Four Gospels to compare the Gospel accounts side by side before judging historical issues. | Falk does not resolve a particular alleged discrepancy in the answer, because the question is general. He recommends Kurt Aland's Synopsis of the Four Gospels as an essential New Testament tool because it places the Gospel accounts side by side and lets the reader compare where each event appears in context. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/-moIej_LjtE?t=11409" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:10:09</a> | Can the Greek word for brother also mean cousin, especially in claims about Mary having children? | Falk says the cousin reading looks like a theological stretch. Greek has a specific word for cousin, and he says the brother language in context is better read as brothers and sisters. | Falk checks the lexical issue and notes that Greek has a specific word for cousin, which makes it harder to claim that the Gospel references simply mean cousins. He allows that words can have extended semantic ranges, but says the relevant brother and sister language in context does not establish the cousin reading. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/-moIej_LjtE?t=11960" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:19:20</a> | Are the Samaritans correct that Mount Gerizim, rather than Zion, was the place chosen by God for worship? | Falk says no. He sees Samaritan worship at Gerizim as later and the Samaritan Pentateuch's Gerizim emphasis as a later amendment, while still valuing the Samaritan Pentateuch as an important source. | Falk dates Samaritan worship at Gerizim to the post-722 BC Assyrian disruption of northern Israel, when populations were moved and local worship was reshaped. He argues that Gerizim's prominence in the Samaritan Pentateuch is a later amendment to justify that worship, while Zion remains tied to the Solomonic temple under the united monarchy. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/-moIej_LjtE?t=12454" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:27:34</a> | What is your reaction to cannabis and cow-dung residue at a temple at Tel Arad, and could it be strange fire? | Falk says cow dung could fit strange fire as an unclean fire source, but he is skeptical about cannabis claims and warns that residue studies can be contaminated or overinterpreted. | Falk says cow dung could qualify as strange fire because it would be fire from an unclean source. He is much more cautious about the cannabis claim, comparing it to dubious residue claims about cocaine and tobacco on mummies and warning that later contamination and handling can mislead residue studies. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/-moIej_LjtE?t=12991" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:36:31</a> | Could you talk about historical errors in the Catholic Apocrypha? | He says the question is too broad to answer well without preparation. Judith is the first example that comes to mind, but he asks for a specific book and advance notice. | Falk says the request is too vague to answer responsibly off the cuff because it asks about a broad body of texts. He mentions Judith, especially chapter 1, as the first example he associates with historical problems, but asks for a specific book and advance notice before treating the topic in detail. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/-moIej_LjtE?t=13195" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:39:55</a> | Have you heard the argument that Jesus is Yahweh but God the Father is Most High and higher than Yahweh? | Falk identifies this as subordinationism and warns that rank language must be carefully defined so it does not become ontological subordination, subjugationism, Arianism, or ditheism. | Falk identifies the view as subordinationism and connects it historically with Origen's rank-language about Father and Son. He says rank or economic language can be acceptable if carefully defined, but warns that if it becomes ontological subordination it moves toward subjugationism, Arianism, or other serious Trinitarian errors. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/-moIej_LjtE?t=13654" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:47:34</a> | Is the face of the Sphinx a Sub-Saharan African? | Falk says no. He says the missing nose makes that impression more plausible, but the restored features and dynasty context point to the Memphis area, not Sub-Saharan Africa. | Falk says the Sphinx can look suggestive of a Sub-Saharan face because the nose is missing, but he argues that restoring the nose and beard changes that impression. He also points to the dynasty's Memphis-area origin and says the monument is not portraying a Sub-Saharan African figure. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/-moIej_LjtE?t=13746" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:49:06</a> | Does Romans 1:18-20 mean everyone propositionally knows God exists, or is the passage more nuanced? | Falk says it is more nuanced. Knowledge of God is available through creation, but sinful humanity corporately suppresses the truth. | Falk says Romans 1 does not mean every individual propositionally knows God exists in the same explicit way. Rather, God's reality is available and made evident through creation, while humanity corporately suppresses that truth in unrighteousness, so some may overlook what is available. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/-moIej_LjtE?t=14090" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:54:50</a> | How many languages are left that still need Bible translation? | Falk says it is getting low, but many remaining languages are critically endangered with very few speakers, so some may die out before they can be fully recorded and translated. | Falk does not give a firm current number beyond saying the total is getting low and reacting to the suggested figure of about 200. He says many remaining languages are critically endangered, sometimes with fewer than 100 speakers or only a handful of elderly speakers, making documentation and translation a race against extinction. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/-moIej_LjtE?t=14367" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:59:27</a> | After the controversies and slander, have you struggled with academia, and how can viewers help? | Falk says the controversy damaged his academic standing despite later vindication and pushed him toward full-time YouTube; he says supporting his publishing work is the best help. | Falk says the slander damaged his academic standing because people remembered the controversy more than the later admissions that he had been taken out of context. He says this helped push him into full-time YouTube, and he identifies support for his publishing efforts as the most direct way viewers can help. |
